data.title: RSPS: Consumer Staples Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly article series reports industry metrics in the consumer staples sector, aiming at a top-down analysis of value, quality and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP) and Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Consumer Staples ETF (NYSEARCA:RSPS), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non-available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for food in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in food companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: Truist Financial: Q1 Earnings And Valuation Prompt A Rating Upgrade
data.description:
Introduction

Major regional bank Truist Financial Corporation (NYSE:TFC) reported its first quarter 2025 results today, which prompted me to take a fresh look at its fundamentals, after my last article was published in January following the release of its full year 2024 results.

Larger competitor U.S. Bancorp (USB) reported solid results yesterday, suggesting Truist could also surprise positively, and indeed I'd say it was a good quarter, even if there were some aspects I wasn't too happy about.

Let's look into how Truist has performed so far in 2025 and whether or not management is adopting a more cautious stance given the significant increase in macroeconomic uncertainties related to the recently announced tariffs and potentially resulting recession.

Truist Q1 2025 Earnings Review

For the first quarter of 2025, Truist reported both adjusted earnings per share (EPS) and net revenues in line with estimates (ignoring the insignificant beat by $0.01 per share and $10 million, respectively).

Adjusted EPS decreased by 3.3% year-over-year and by 4.4% sequentially. Return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) was 12.3% in Q1 2025, a decrease of 0.6 percentage points compared to the fourth quarter and a decrease of 4.0 percentage points year-over-year. The year-over-year decrease is largely due to the significant increase in tangible common equity ($39.9 billion compared to $28.9 billion, see comparison on p. 3 of the Q1 2025 performance summary).

I regard the decline in EPS as somewhat disappointing, particularly in view of the ongoing share buybacks. On a sequential basis, share buybacks have improved the EPS comparison by around 0.7 percentage points. Comparing the weighted average diluted shares outstanding in Q1 2024 with the latest quarterly figure, share buybacks represented a 1.7 percentage point tailwind.

Underlying earnings therefore declined by almost 5% year-over-year. However, on a GAAP basis, earnings increased by 7.4% year-over-year, or 5.6% if the impact of share buybacks is excluded. 2024 GAAP EPS benefited from the sale of the remaining stake in Truist Insurance Holdings, partially offset by the FDIC special assessment and Truist Insurance related stock-based compensation, or SBC, expense. However, the difference between GAAP and non-GAAP results in Q1 2025 was negligible.

So what are the reasons for the decline in earnings per share in Q1 2025?

The most obvious reason is lower net revenues (-3.2% quarter-over-quarter) due to a 2.4% decline in net interest income, which in turn is partly due to a slight decrease in net interest margin (-6 bps quarter-over-quarter, Figure 1). I wouldn't go so far as to call Truist's current performance problematic in this regard, but it's still a disappointing performance given the modest margin expansion reported yesterday by peer U.S. Bancorp (USB). That said, Truist is actually more profitable in a direct comparison - by 29 bps in Q1 2025.

Although the decline in the net interest margin is somewhat disappointing, I would argue that Truist's deposit and borrowing costs are well controlled. As Figure 2 shows, the average cost of deposits has fallen again, by 10 bps to a very respectable 1.79%. Borrowing costs have continued to normalize with respect to maturity, with short-term borrowings down 32 bps to 4.49% and long-term borrowings essentially unchanged (-1 bp) at 5.05%. To put these figures into perspective, it should be noted that deposits accounted for 88% of the total of deposits and borrowings, while short-term and long-term borrowings accounted for 5% and 7%, respectively.

I found it reassuring that average loans and leases were up 1.1% compared to the prior quarter (Figure 2), broadly in line with its larger competitor, U.S. Bancorp. However, it is worth noting that Truist's longer-term performance in this regard has lagged well behind that of USB (see Figure 3 in my previous article). Loan growth was noticeable in most of Truist's lending areas, particularly commercial and industrial, residential mortgage, and indirect auto loans.

Weakness in noninterest income was another, albeit less significant, factor of contribution to the year-over-year decline in adjusted EPS. Non-interest income fell 5.3% quarter-over-quarter (-3.7% year-over-year), although it should be noted that unlike U.S. Bancorp - where noninterest income currently accounts for more than 40% of total net revenue - this income stream contributed only 28% of Truist's total revenue in the first quarter.

The main reason for the decline was a $69 million decline in income from solar equity investments (among others). Overall, this position was responsible for 5.0% and 9.4% of noninterest income in Q1 2025 and Q4 2024, respectively. Other weak points were income from investment banking and trading (-15.5% year-over-year) and income from operating leases (-10.2% year-over-year). However, as these areas only accounted for 6.6% of total revenues, I am not really worried - also because investment banking and trading revenues increased by 4.2% compared to Q4 2024.

From the perspective of comparing revenues and expenses, it was also reassuring to see Truist report positive operating leverage for Q1 2025 - just like U.S. Bancorp, which reported its third consecutive quarter of positive operating leverage yesterday. Truist's noninterest expenses were down 5.4% and 4.3% quarter-over-quarter on an adjusted and unadjusted basis, respectively, with the key drivers being professional fees and outside processing (-12% QoQ, 13% of Q1 expenses, the area I had my eye on previously), equipment expenses (-30% QoQ, 6% of Q1 expenses), and net occupancy expenses (-9% QoQ, 3% of Q1 expenses). And while I already found U.S. Bancorp's 2% year-over-year decline in compensation expense to be pretty impressive, the 2.6% decline reported today by Truist (unchanged from the prior quarter) was a really bright spot in the earnings report. After all, personnel costs currently account for around 55% of Truist's total noninterest expenses.

As a result of what I consider to be good cost management, Truist's efficiency ratio has improved again, both on a GAAP and adjusted basis (Figure 4). Nevertheless, an efficiency ratio of 56.4% still indicates considerable scope for future improvement.

Looking at deposits, after yesterday's report from U.S. Bancorp, it was not surprising that Truist also reported a decline in noninterest bearing deposits (-1.9% quarter-over-quarter). However, unlike USB, Truist reported a net increase in deposits, marking the second consecutive quarter of growth (Figure 5). Time deposits rose by a strong 9.4% compared to the fourth quarter, although it should be noted that they only account for 10% of total deposits. Other interest-bearing deposits (interest checking, money market and savings accounts) remained unchanged overall compared to the previous quarter.

Finally, a look at Truist's asset quality metrics, such as the nonperforming assets ratio (Figure 6) and the charge-off ratio, leaves a continued positive impression. After all, charge-offs have risen only marginally compared to the fourth quarter and remain below the national average, likely also in the first quarter (Figure 7). Nonperforming assets have risen slightly, but at less than 0.5% of total loans and leases, I am far from concerned. By comparison, U.S. Bancorp's NPA ratio is only a few basis points lower, and the bank has long been known for its conservative underwriting practices.

Truist repurchased shares for another $500 million in Q1 2025, reducing the number of shares outstanding by 0.7% compared to Q4 2024. While the healthy asset base and reasonably solid profitability make it only reasonable to continue buybacks on such a scale, I would expect a moderation given the significant increase in macroeconomic uncertainty.

However, Truist management emphasized on the earnings call that $500 million had already been repurchased in the second quarter, which does indeed signal confidence. This view is backed up by the fact that management is targeting further buybacks of up to $250 million in the second quarter.

Personally, I am somewhat on the fence. As a conservative, long-term investor, I wouldn't mind a more conservative approach to share buybacks in the current environment. However, from a valuation perspective, and also in view of a CET1 (Common Equity Tier 1) ratio of 11.3%, I think the current share buybacks are indeed rather opportunistic: buying back shares at a blended price-to-earnings ratio of 10 (Figure 8), a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 9, a price-to-book ratio of 0.74 and a price-to-tangible book ratio of 1.04 (Figure 9) certainly leaves the impression of money well spent.

Conclusion

Truist Financial Corporation presented a generally solid earnings report for the first quarter of 2025. Growth in both loans and deposits was encouraging, albeit slow as expected, but the key takeaway was the bank's disciplined expense management. Non-interest expenses declined 4.3% on a GAAP basis and 5.4% on an adjusted basis compared to the prior quarter, resulting in positive operating leverage for the first quarter - despite the 3.2% sequential decline in net revenues. As a result of the disciplined expense management, Truist's efficiency ratio improved to 59.3% and 56.4% on a GAAP and non-GAAP basis, respectively.

The bank's asset quality remains solid, as evidenced by stable nonperforming assets ratio (0.48%, virtually in line with peer U.S. Bancorp) and net charge-offs of 0.6% of total loans and leases - also in line with its larger competitor and about 8 to 10 bps below the national average.

With that in mind, I wasn't too surprised that management continued to buy back shares at a healthy pace in the first quarter. However, it was indeed somewhat surprising to see another $500 million spent on share repurchases in the second quarter, which began just two and a half weeks ago. Given the precipitous drop in TFC's share price (in line with other banks) in response to Trump's "Liberation Day" speech and subsequent announcements, I think this is a shrewd move given the bank's strong capital base. Indeed, the fact that management has indicated that another $250 million could be spent on buybacks this quarter signals confidence in the bank's fundamentals. With a price-to-earnings ratio of 10 (9 based on 2025 EPS consensus) and a price-to-book ratio of less than 0.8, I would say that this is indeed money well spent.

That said, and despite the undoubtedly solid credit quality metrics and healthy capitalization, I really wouldn't mind if management scaled back buybacks in the third quarter to maintain a strong loss-absorbing capacity.

All in all, I believe it is appropriate to upgrade TFC stock to "buy," primarily due to its attractive valuation and progress on the cost side of the equation. However, I intend to keep TFC as a comparatively small position in my portfolio for risk management reasons, to the tune of 0.75% of my portfolio value in terms of invested capital.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: The Prospects For Silver And The SLVP ETF
data.description:
Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) has a long and volatile history as a means of exchange, an industrial commodity, and a speculative metal that has experienced explosive and implosive price action when a herd of buyers or sellers, or a single buyer such as the Hunt Brothers in the 1970s, attempt to corner the market. Silver has taken two trips to either side of the $50 per ounce level, in 1980 and 2011, with both failing. While the 1980 $50.36 high led to a 1993 $3.51 low, the 2011 $49.82 peak led to a higher 2020, pandemic-inspired $11.64 per ounce low. Since 2020, silver has made higher lows and higher highs. In March 2025, the price reached $35.495, the highest level since early 2012.

Silver’s most recent 21.8% rally from the December 19, 2024, $29.145 low to the March 28, 2025, $35.495 high occurred as gold reached higher and higher new record peaks. Moreover, the tariff threat and uncertainty caused physical silver to flow from Europe to the U.S., tightening the London wholesale market and causing prices to rise on the futures and physical market. On April 2, the day U.S. President Trump declared “Liberation Day,” the tariff announcements removed the silver market’s uncertainty as the tariff protocols excluded silver, copper, and other precious and base metals. Prices dropped like a stone, with silver falling 22.4% from $35.495 on March 28 to a $27.545 low on April 7.

Distortions caused by uncertainty over tariffs caused the iShares MSCI Global Silver and Metals Miners ETF (BATS:SLVP) product to reach a high of $15.79 per share on March 18 and a decline of 21.5% to a $12.40 low on April 7. SLVP has done an excellent job tracking silver prices. The ETF’s history suggests that if an explosive move in silver is on the horizon, SLVP could be a valuable proxy for the precious metal or other silver-related assets.

I last wrote about SLVP on Seeking Alpha in July 2024, when I concluded:

Silver is consolidating after breaking above the critical technical resistance level. SLVP could offer incredible value at the $12.25 per share level if silver is on its way to challenge the 2011 and 1980 highs.

Silver was trading at $30.915 on July 9, 2014, with the SLVP ETF at the $12.34 per share level. At $32.405 on April 17, 2025, active-month silver futures were 4.82% higher. SLVP at $16.18 per share was over 30% higher, outperforming the precious metal.

Silver is gold’s sibling: Silver’s price is more volatile than gold

Gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) have moved to new nominal record highs in January, February, March, and April 2025.

The monthly chart highlights gold’s ascent to the latest all-time high. Meanwhile, gold’s rally has been steady, making monthly historical volatility 12.55%.

Wild price swings in the silver futures market result is nearly twice gold’s historical volatility at 23.52%.

Gold and silver have many of the same monetary characteristics, but while gold is an investment metal, silver is highly speculative. The $50 level in 1980 and 2011 attracted many speculative investors when the price was trending higher, and when the price failed, significant long liquidation added to price variance.

Silver demand has been rising: Investment and speculative demand drive the price

The Silver Institute, a trade organization supported by silver producers and silver-related businesses, said in a late January 2025 report that while global silver demand is forecast stable at 1.20 billion ounces, silver supply is expected to grow by 3% to an eleven-year high of 1.05 billion ounces, resulting in a 150 million ounce supply-demand deficit.

Meanwhile, investment demand is the crucial factor determining silver’s path of least resistance. The bullish trend since the 2020 pandemic-inspired low below $12 suggests that gold’s bullish trend suggests that silver’s speculative demand will remain robust in 2025, which could add to the deficit and drive the price higher.

A technical break in 2024 and Q1 could lead to a parabolic move

Silver futures eclipsed two technical resistance levels as the silver market experienced significant tightness caused by the uncertainty of tariffs, with metal flowing from Europe to the U.S.

The twenty-year chart highlights silver’s October 2024 $35.07 high. Silver eclipsed the 2024 in March 2025, reaching $35.495, which was also above the October 2012 $35.445 peak. The next technical resistance level stands at the February 2012 $37.58 high. Moves above the 2012 peak could ignite a speculative buying frenzy in the silver market.

Silver mining stocks are highly speculative

The top holdings of the iShares MSCI Global Silver and Metals Miners ETF include:

SLVP holds a diversified portfolio of the leading silver mining companies. At $16.18 per share, SLVP had $286.91 million in assets under management. SLVP trades an average of over 200,000 shares daily and charges a 0.39% management fee.

SLVP’s Seeking Alpha ETF Grades show an A+ in momentum because of the bullish trend in silver mining shares. In late 2024, SLVP paid a 16 cents per share dividend, translating to a 0.99% yield, earning a C- in dividends.

The assets and volume earn SILJ a B in liquidity, while the highly volatile silver market receives a D- in the risk category. The 0.38% management fee gets a B- in expenses.

SLVP’s price action from 2020-2021 could be a model for the coming months

SLVP kept pace with silver during the most recent correction. Silver futures fell 22.4% from the March 28 high to the April 7 low. Over around the same period, SLVP dropped 21.5%.

If a parabolic move in silver is on the horizon, the price action in silver and the SLVP ETF from 2020 through 2021 could be a model.

The monthly silver futures chart highlights the 160.7% move from $11.64 in March 2020 to $30.35 per ounce in February 2021.

Over the same period, SLVP rose 247% from $5.62 to $19.50 per share, substantially outperforming silver futures.

It may take some time for the silver market to unwind the metal flows that sent significant quantities of the precious metal from Europe to the United States, but the trend remains bullish. As gold makes higher highs, it may be only a matter of time before silver continues its ascent, eclipsing the next upside target at the February 2012 $37.58 high. If silver prices experience a parabolic move, the price action from 2020-2021 suggests the SLVP ETF could outperform the futures price on a percentage basis on the upside. Meanwhile, buying silver and silver mining stocks on corrective dips has been optimal over the past years, and I expect that to continue.

Demystify commodities in an era of uncertainty

Commodities experienced significant volatility in 2024, with energy prices impacted by geopolitical tensions, while precious metals and industrial metals saw significant gains. Looking ahead to 2025, the outlook remains murky, with trade policies shaping future commodity performance. During these times, you need someone who can share their knowledge and expertise to position for success.

The Hecht Commodity Report, led by seasoned Wall Street veteran Andrew Hecht, offers in-depth, actionable insights into everything from oil, gold, and grains to currencies and industrial metals. Subscribers receive weekly commentary, trade alerts, and forward-looking strategies and a proven approach that has helped investors navigate complex markets for decades, providing clear, actionable buy and sell recommendations.

Start your risk-free trial today and lock in the current price before it increases to $999 on April 30, 2025.

data.title: Palantir Stock: The Breakthrough That Changes Everything
data.description:
When we think of AI stocks, NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) is, without a doubt, the biggest winner in recent time thanks to an insatiable demand for its high-performance chips and GPUs - essential for the development of AI. However, if you’ve missed the proverbial boat and want to invest in another company making waves in AI, then look no further than Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR).

While NVIDIA has been dominating the headlines with their latest hardware, tariffs, and export restrictions, Palantir has been busy building impressive AI software platforms that run on NVIDIA’s hardware. The widespread adoption of AI software platforms and solutions is just starting, and Palantir is at the forefront.

Spoiler alert: I’m initiating a “Strong Buy” rating on the stock.

Quick Overview

Palantir Technologies is a data analytics company and is a relatively new one - it went public in 2020 and has made great strides with its data integration capabilities through its four leading software platforms:

  • Gotham: Helps intelligence and defense agencies identify patterns in vast datasets.

  • Foundry: The central operating system for enterprise data

  • Apollo: A cloud-based deployment system for continuous operation of critical systems; and...

  • Palantir Artificial Intelligence Platform (AIP): Integrates large language models and other AI technologies into organizations' existing workflows.

Initially, Palantir focused on U.S. government contracts to aid counterterrorism and intelligence. However, the company faced controversy surrounding its surveillance work and has since expanded to commercial sectors like healthcare, finance, manufacturing, and more.

Palantir Technologies' Recent Financials

Palantir Technologies’ Q4 2024 financials reported impressive numbers despite some setbacks. Revenue increased 36% year-over-year to $827.5 million, reflecting a strong market demand for their data analytics solutions.

However, this was offset by increased operating expenses, which resulted in its quarterly net income (after all expenses) diving 18% to $76.9 million. The bright spot was its reported adjusted EPS increasing 75% to $0.14 - beating the consensus by 23.72%.

There’s a lot to be optimistic about when it comes to Palantir’s FY 2024 numbers. Its top-line grew 29% to $2.87 billion, while its bottom-line grew 120% to $462 million, and translated to adjusted EPS surging 64% to $0.41 - outpacing the consensus by 7.04%.

Bottom line: Palantir’s FY 2025 numbers demonstrate that the company’s long-term strategies deliver results.

One issue that’s common with growing companies like Palantir is their balance sheets. In Palantir’s case, there are notable improvements. Although its total liabilities for FY 2024 increased 29% year-over-year to approximately $1.25 billion, the company’s total assets grew by 40% to $6.34 billion - a positive sign for the company's overall health, though a significant increase in both these figured could raise concerns for investors worried about the company’s cost of growth.

PLTR Stock Price Performance

PLTR’s price performance is also nothing short of remarkable; the stock gained 349.32% over the past year. The stock started surging as demands for Palantir AI platforms began to take off, hitting its 52-week high of $125.41 in February.

However, PLTR now trades at $98.40, which is 21.5% off from its 52-week high. What happened? Quite a bit, and it’s not what you might think.

According to some reports, the pullback started with CEO Alex Karp’s plans of selling upward of 10 million of his own shares, after an already-significant stock sale, triggering fears about how the executive feels about the company. But here’s the thing: executive stock sales are often routine liquidity events rather than votes of “no confidence.” Even Mark Zuckerberg routinely sells shares that he owns.

Another potential reason, and I believe a more impactful one, is that U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth is directing Pentagon officials to prepare for potential 8% annual defense budget cuts over 5 years, which, unsurprisingly, also raised concerns about Palantir's U.S. government contract revenue.

To me, however, I think the recent pullback potentially presents a buying opportunity, at the least. There’s been a ton of volatility this first half of April 2025, with, at one point, more than $10 trillion of market capitalization vanishing. In fact, there was a point where 3-4% swings in the index were the norm; not exactly the kind of rollercoaster that I like riding.

5-Year Revenue Comparison Vs. Peers

At the moment, Palantir's 5-YR revenue is still conservative compared to the mature tech giants like Salesforce and Adobe, but remember, Palantir is still “embryonic” compared to its peer group.

Palantir Technologies is working expanding its market position beyond government, and when we look at the change in percent over the last five years, the numbers tell a compelling story.

In terms of revenue growth, Palantir is #2 among the group. In fact, with a 163.3% revenue growth rate over the period, Palantir positions itself as one of the strongest performers among this peer group, second only to AppLovin.

And, although government contracts have always been Palantir’s figurative “bread and butter”, I think its commercial sector expansion is the primary driver in its valuation expansion.

Reasons For My Strong Buy Rating On Palantir

Although PLTR stock currently trades -21% off from its 52-week high, there are several reasons to buy the dip, beyond the fact that the stock is trading at a discount.

First, Palantir’s annual and quarterly revenue increased by 29% and 36% year-over-year. If the company maintains this upward trajectory - which I think they will - investors can expect these figures to improve in the following years.

Second, since the company’s overall performance was driven by a 43% year-over-year increase in clients, it proves Palantir’s expansion beyond government contracts (into the private sector) is maturing well and will translate to further revenue growth.

Third, the company projects 31% revenue growth for FY 2025. And since Palantir's business model comes from recurring subscriptions, they’ll be in a good place to use that income to reduce new customer acquisition costs (CAC) which will translate to further improvements in margins, and of course, an increase in the company's overall value.

Lastly, I think the most understated catalyst for Palantir is its ongoing partnerships with many organizations, which could bring tremendous long-term growth potential.

Recently, Everfox and Palantir announced a partnership combining Everfox’s cross-domain solutions with Palantir’s AI-powered data analysis tools. The partnership is said to improve war fighters’ joint command and control capabilities in classified network environments.

Moreover, Palantir partnered with R1 to launch an AI lab called R3 - designed to improve healthcare revenue cycle management. The collaboration will address the ~$160 billion spent annually on revenue cycle management, and this is expected to improve cash flow for healthcare organizations.

The initial deployment of R3 to enterprise customers is expected in late 2025 and if healthcare organizations use this solution, Palantir’s revenue should soar.

What The Analysts Say About Palantir

Wedbush is currently one of Palantir’s biggest advocates, despite rumored U.S. defense budget cuts.

According to Wedbush, “The bears… now have found their latest ‘silver bullet’ negative thesis around PLTR being exposed to these budget cuts. This is exactly the opposite of how we believe these DOD cuts will play out.”

In fact, the firm maintains its Outperform rating and $120 price target on PLTR stock, rejecting worries about defense budget cuts and said Palantir will actually benefit from changes in Pentagon spending.

And just because the U.S. government isn’t expanding doesn’t mean others won’t. Case in point: NATO’s recent contract with Palantir, acquiring the Maven Smart System. Should this product work well, I wouldn’t be surprised if we saw Maven deployed to other NATO countries.

On the topic of Wedbush, they also consider Project Stargate (a government AI initiative) another growth driver for the company. The firm stated, “Karp&Co. are playing chess in the AI Arms Race while others play checkers” and even suggested that as AI implementation speeds up, Palantir might evolve something comparable to Salesforce.

The Risks To Palantir’s Business Model

So far, I’ve laid out the case for why I like Palantir stock - and there’s lots to like. However, my thesis wouldn’t be complete without a risk analysis.

While it’s hard to know where a business will be in 1 year from now, let alone 5 or 10, in Palantir’s case, roughly 50% of their revenue comes from the U.S. Government. Furthermore, transitioning to a more scalable, subscription-based model - which I think should form the backbone of any company - is going to be difficult. Selling to governments is very different from selling B2B.

And should Palantir be successful with their push in the B2B space, contract sizes and pricing will need to be adjusted to be competitive. If this were six months ago, I’d say only the U.S. Government has a near-unlimited credit limit - businesses certainly do not.

There’s also geopolitical issues to consider as well. Considering Palantir already sells to multinationals operating in China, it’s entirely possible that the U.S. may ban its use, directly or indirectly. Airbus, for example, operates in Tianjin, yet is a long term Palantir client.

And finally, Palantir stock may be considered expensive. They are not as mature a company as say Intuit, or Adobe, but these companies were “small” once as well.

Given Palantir’s appetite for growth, I’d say these risks are real, but certainly manageable.

PLTR Stock: Current Valuation

167.26

Palantir's stock trading currently trades at 167.26x, 2025 forward P/E, and might appear expensive. It towers over the rest of its industry peers and the sector by a staggering margin, which means the stock likely reflects aggressive growth assumptions. But, a multiple like this is not uncommon for newer companies. Tesla, for example, was trading at 1100x just 5 years ago amid considerable excitement about the company’s prospects.

However, my impression is that Palantir's strengths are still highly undervalued: its AI platform (AIP) is gaining notable traction across industries, it's successfully diversifying from the U.S. government to other government entities and commercial sectors, its data integration capabilities provide a competitive moat, and all of Palantir's potentially lucrative partnerships across different sectors.

Also, Palantir's consistent revenue growth stems from a strong market validation of its offerings, which means the company is set to capitalize on the demand for AI-powered data analytics solutions.

Target Price

Palantir is still in its infancy compared to its peers. Companies like Adobe, Salesforce, and Intuit no longer have the power to achieve the exponential growth potential that Palantir currently has.

Given Palantir’s new relationship with NATO - which I think is a milestone for the company, I can see them exceeding the consensus 2034 EPS target of $5.80 per share. As a result, I’ve set a price target of $174 on PLTR stock, representing a multiple of 30x of its 2034 $5.80 EPS estimate, reasonable for a mature software company. In fact, companies like Intuit, Microsoft (MSFT), and Autodesk (ADSK) all trade with a similar 30x forward P/E.

Last, should Palantir’s EPS get revised up thanks to working with some or all of the 32 other NATO members, I think in 10 years $174 could be a discount.

data.title: WidePoint's Valuation Disconnect Looks Like An Entry Signal
data.description:
Thesis

WidePoint Corporation (NYSE: NYSE:WYY) is a federally focused IT services firm specializing in secure mobile management, identity authentication, and telecom lifecycle solutions for high-compliance environments.

From my perspective, despite the stock trading down sharply in the past month, and in the wake of its latest earnings release, the market is overlooking a company that is quietly transitioning from operational stabilization to scalable growth. With improving cash flows, deep federal contract visibility, and strategic positioning in a high-barrier ecosystem, I see this pullback not as a sign of weakness, but as a potential inflection point for a long-underappreciated company in government tech infrastructure.

Profitability Within Reach

WidePoint is one of those oddly compelling contradictions you sometimes find in the federal tech world, a company that, by all appearances, is getting stronger in exactly the ways you'd hope, but whose route to actually turning a profit still seems to hover just beyond reach.

That's not meant as a criticism, but more a nod to the dilemma that quite a few investors seem to be wrestling with. How do you go about valuing a company that's got strong revenue growth momentum since 2021, long-term contract clarity, and a decent strategic foothold, but still ends up losing money? In WidePoint's case, the answer seems to be leaning more toward the optimistic side, even if a few warning signs haven't entirely gone away.

Let's begin with what's going right. WidePoint's fourth-quarter and full-year 2024 results delivered something you don't see all that often in the small-cap tech world these days: positive surprises that actually have some momentum behind them. Annual revenue jumped 35% to $142.6 million, coming in ahead of both the company's own guidance and what analysts had expected.

Adjusted EBITDA jumped 229% to $2.6 million, and free cash flow swung from a loss of $300,000 to a gain of $2.5 million, a shift of 933%. The company has now posted positive free cash flow for five quarters in a row, and positive adjusted EBITDA for thirty straight quarters. That's a pattern.

What's more interesting is where that revenue is actually coming from. It's not the usual burst of SaaS license sales from volatile, venture-backed startups. Instead, it's grounded in long-term federal contracts, with $290 million in backlog at the end of the year, not counting the $25 million Spiral 4 award from a U.S. Department of Defense combat support agency. These aren't the kind of contracts you get from meeting regulatory requirements, knowing the field, and showing you can deliver. WidePoint's recent FedRAMP authorization for its Intelligent Technology Management System (ITMS) is a significant development on that front. It places the company among a small group of providers cleared for high-trust, high-security federal cloud contracts, giving it a solid footing for upcoming opportunities like DHS CWMS 3.0, NASA SEWP VI, and the large-scale Alliant 3 procurement cycle.

Let's talk about what this actually means in practice. Take the DHS CWMS 3.0 recompete, for example. This is the third iteration of a massive wireless services contract from the Department of Homeland Security. It covers everything from mobile device lifecycle management to secure communications across one of the most sprawling and sensitive government agencies. DHS needs a vendor with embedded security protocols, track records, and FedRAMP compliance. WidePoint checks those boxes now, and it's already the incumbent.

Then there's SEWP VI, NASA's government-wide IT contract, which comes with an expected ceiling in the tens of billions. It's one of the key vehicles for federal spending on cloud services, cybersecurity, telecom, and endpoint management, and it's used by just about every agency you can name. If WidePoint secures a spot on SEWP VI, it would give the company steady access to pre-approved funding from dozens of departments. And it's worth noting, that only a small group of providers even qualify for it.

Alliant 3, meanwhile, is the crown jewel. It's the GSA's next-generation IT services contract, with a projected ceiling of $75 billion, covering everything from standard IT work to newer fields like AI, machine learning, and quantum computing. If WidePoint is seriously in the running for it, that's a clear sign they've stepped up to a higher tier.

And it's worth considering what this looks like in the broader picture. With companies like Okta (OKTA) and AT&T (T) outperforming the S&P 500 in 2025 and pulling investor focus toward cybersecurity and managed mobility, WidePoint is in that general vicinity, positioned at the crossroads of secure mobile device management, identity infrastructure, and federal compliance. Okta's outperformance reinforces that secure identity isn't optional, it's foundational.

AT&T's run shows that mobile infrastructure is gaining in complexity and budget priority. WidePoint may not match their scale, but it services the granular, compliance-driven, operational layers they can't easily reach.

On the other end of the spectrum, Cognizant's (CTSH) flatlining YTD reflects the vulnerability of generalized IT providers exposed to commercial cycles. WidePoint, in contrast, is leaning into the defensive posture investors are hungry for right now.

Recession Risk Context

That said, it wouldn't be honest to call this a straightforward story. The wider market environment adds more context to where WidePoint fits into all of this. TS Lombard has been warning that recession risks are being underestimated, and with fiscal policy becoming less predictable in the Trump administration's second term, a lot of investors are beginning to pull back from cyclical plays. In that light, WidePoint's contracts, like that newly secured Spiral 4 deal, stand out as steady, federally backed revenue sources. They're tied to essential infrastructure that keeps running, no matter what the economy is doing.

Meanwhile, the company continues to operate at a GAAP loss, of $1.9 million for the year, or -$0.21 per share, though that's a sharp improvement from 2023's $4 million loss. Q4 came in at -$0.04 per share, which was better than expected but still in the red. That net loss, combined with only a modest cash build, $6.8 million in cash on hand at year-end, flat with 2023, makes it harder for some investors to get comfortable. Unbilled receivables tied to federal customers are being worked through, but it underscores how fragile the working capital dynamics can be for a company of this scale operating in a heavily bureaucratic environment.

Turning to margins, on the surface, gross profit margin dropped to 13% from 15% last year, mostly because carrier services, those useful but not especially lucrative beasts, pumped in $28.6 million in revenue while contributing next to nothing to the bottom line. Take those out of the picture, though, and things start to look a bit more respectable: the core business posted a gross margin of 36% in Q4, up from 32% the year before.

And then there's the matter of guidance, which isn't really there.

Management was clear enough about wanting to hit positive EPS in 2025, but they stopped short of giving actual revenue or EBITDA targets, saying they need more time to update forecasts based on how Q1 is shaping up. Fair enough, but it does leave a void, particularly for institutional investors who like to have something, anything concrete to plug into their models. Meanwhile, even though the federal pipeline looks promising, the ongoing consolidation of procurement under the GSA and some rumblings about cutting back on non-statutory FAR clauses could end up making WidePoint's usual contract routes somewhat bumpier than usual.

Cash Burn and Cash Runway

Let's take a closer look at cash flow. By the end of 2024, WidePoint had just under $7 million in cash on hand. During that time, it generated roughly $1.7 million in free cash flow, which means the company isn't burning through cash, it's actually operating with a positive cash flow. That said, if we conservatively excluded working capital improvements and smoothed for operational volatility, a more neutral baseline free cash flow would suggest minimal net cash consumption at worst.

Still, if WidePoint were to return to a modest monthly cash burn of around $0.3 million, which lines up with earlier periods of net outflows, it would have enough cash for a cash runway to last roughly 22 to 24 months. That estimate doesn't factor in any additional liquidity from its existing $4 million revolving credit line or potential new contract awards.

Even so, it's worth not emphasizing that WidePoint's small market capitalization, just under $25 million, along with its fairly limited daily trading volume, introduces some structural liquidity concerns. These elements, while not uncommon in the microcap space, can make it more difficult for institutional investors to engage meaningfully and can, under the right, or wrong, conditions, lead to sharper price swings.

Valuation and Growth Potential

The company has made it clear that achieving positive EPS in 2025 is a top strategic goal. Consensus analyst estimates support this trajectory, projecting EPS growth of 42.86% in 2025 and 91.67% in 2026.

The free cash flow numbers do a solid job of backing up the optimistic view. Levered free cash flow rose 146.91% year-over-year, which is well ahead of the Information Technology sector's median of 15.29%.

Operating cash flow jumped even more, 168.98% over the year, outpacing sector norms by more than tenfold. And yet, despite all that cash being generated, WidePoint wrapped up 2024 with pretty much the same cash balance it started with: that $6.8 million. The reason? A chunk of that money is still caught up in unbilled receivables and the slow-turning gears of federal contract administration, things the company says it's addressing.

Still, with only $4.94 million in debt and a modest enterprise value of $23.04 million, the company looks financially stable and unlikely to need to raise dilutive capital anytime soon.

Against the broader backdrop of the IT consulting and services industry, forecast to grow at a CAGR of 8.38% through 2030, WidePoint's niche positioning in secure telecom lifecycle management and federal cybersecurity gives it an edge. The IT consulting segment itself is forecast to grow at 13.3% CAGR in 2029, driven by digital transformation, cloud adoption, and enterprise security spending. WidePoint, with its recent FedRAMP authorization and a contract backlog, is in a strong position to capitalize on this tailwind, especially in the high-trust, high-barrier federal procurement space.

According to Seeking Alpha, WidePoint's revenue growth is outpacing the sector by more than 500%, and its forward EBITDA growth is estimated at 57.4%, compared to a sector median of 9.47%. Working capital also climbed 75.71% year-over-year, pointing to a stronger liquidity position that could support further growth.

Final Takeaway

I would rate WidePoint a "Buy". While the company's continued GAAP losses and lack of near-term guidance create hesitation, the strengthening fundamentals, improving cash flow profile, and deep federal contract backlog signal a business nearing a meaningful inflection point. With the stock trading sharply lower pre-market despite operational momentum, I see this dislocation as a tactical opportunity rather than a red flag.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Abandonment Risk And Its Impact On Retirement Savings
data.description:
Retirement savings is a critical financial goal that many individuals strive to achieve throughout their working lives. As people live longer and face increasing economic uncertainties, investors cannot overstate the importance of building a robust retirement portfolio. However, a significant threat to this objective is the concept of "retirement savings abandonment risk." This risk can lead to premature withdrawals and discontinuation of contributions, jeopardizing long-term financial security. Understanding and mitigating this risk is essential for reaching retirement goals.

What is retirement savings abandonment risk?

Retirement savings abandonment risk refers to the likelihood that individuals will stop contributing to their retirement accounts or withdraw funds prematurely. This risk is particularly concerning in volatile market conditions when investors may react emotionally to downturns. Most retirement portfolios are designed to balance market risks, such as interest rate and equity risk, with shortfall and longevity risks—the risk that your savings will be too small to finance your retirement or that you will outlive your savings. While addressing these risks is vital for a successful retirement outcome, it often requires taking on additional market risk. Unfortunately, this can inadvertently introduce abandonment risk.

Risks for retirement savers

Market risk: refers to how investments react to market volatility. It is also synonymous with abandonment risk or the risk that participants will abandon their investment if they feel a negative experience is too severe.

Inflation risk: the risk of reduced purchasing power due to rapidly rising prices. Longevity risk: the risk of an individual outliving their savings, either from a savings shortfall or living longer than planned.

Shortfall risk: the risk of not having enough savings to last throughout retirement.

From Principal’s research, we can observe this behavioral finance phenomenon when investors exit their retirement plans during market downturns, typically opting for safer, cash-like investments at the worst possible time. Abandoning a retirement investment strategy at the market's lowest point can have severe consequences, ultimately jeopardizing an investor's retirement goals. The implications of abandoning a long-term investment strategy can ripple through a retiree's financial life, leading to a loss of potential growth and increased reliance on social safety nets.

Common scenarios where abandonment occurs

At Principal Asset Management, our proprietary research reveals several behavioral and emotional factors that contribute to abandonment risk, including:

  1. Loss aversion: Research shows that investors tend to feel the pain of losses more acutely than the pleasure of equivalent gains. This can lead to rash decisions during market downturns.
  2. Myopic risk aversion: Individuals often focus too heavily on short-term market fluctuations, overshadowing their long-term investment objectives. This is especially true for those nearing or in retirement, who may panic and withdraw during a downturn.
  3. Recency bias: Investors frequently overweight recent market performance, which can skew their decision-making. Investors may be more inclined to abandon their savings plans if the market performs poorly.
  4. Flight to safety reflex: During times of volatility, the emotional response to seek safety in cash-like investments can lead to premature withdrawals, further exacerbating the problem.

What drives abandonment risk?

Studies in behavioral finance indicate that risk tolerance varies with age. Younger investors may be more willing to take risks, but as individuals approach retirement age, their risk tolerance typically decreases. When faced with a market downturn, older investors may abandon their plans for perceived safer investments, increasing their risk of financial shortfalls as they cross into and live in retirement.

Based on our proprietary research, different age groups exhibit varying risk tolerance levels. The risk of abandoning retirement plans increases as individuals approach retirement, peaking at the time of retirement. Historically, we’ve observed a rise in the abandonment of retirement plans during significant market downturns, which is detrimental for participants.

Our projections for future abandonment risk are grounded in a 2.0 to 2.25 standard deviation shift alongside our updated annual portfolio risk and return assessments. For younger investors (ages 25-50), we identify a critical threshold at a -25.9% annualized return over two years. For those nearing retirement (ages 50-65), this threshold is -13.5% over the same period. Finally, for retirees (ages 65 and older), we consider a -9.0% annualized return over two years as the critical point.

Once investors have abandoned their retirement plans in favor of safer investments, re-entering the market can be psychologically and emotionally challenging. That’s especially true after a missed market rebound, which may create a cycle of lost opportunities and reduced growth potential

Abandonment risk often leads to a "buy high, sell low" scenario, which can dramatically alter the trajectory of retirement outcomes. If investors abandon their plans at the wrong time, they may face increased shortfall and longevity risks, significantly impacting their quality of life during retirement. Asset allocators must consider these factors to provide retirement investors the best chance for success.

Understanding how abandonment risk affects retirees differently

Research indicates that abandonment risk varies across age groups. Studies show that younger investors are more likely to remain invested during downturns, while older investors are more prone to withdraw funds. Understanding these dynamics is essential for developing targeted education and strategies to mitigate this risk effectively.

  • Younger retirement savers: Generally, younger investors have a higher risk tolerance and can withstand large market drawdowns. Their invested capital is typically smaller, and their human capital (the present value of future earnings) is much higher. This means they have time on their side to recover from market fluctuations.

Risk tolerance changes over time

Investor stage

Abandonment risk assumption*

Early accumulating (Age 25-50)

-26%

Approaching retirement (Age 51-65)

In retirement (Age 65+)

Source: Principal Asset Allocation. For illustrative purposes only. *Based on annualized returns and our modeling assumptions. Our expectations for future abandonment are based on a 2.00 to 2.25 standard deviation move coupled with our blended ex-ante portfolio risk and return assumptions, updated annually. For early accumulating investors (approximately ages 25-50) we have assumed that “pain point” to be at -25.9% annualized portfolio return over a two-year period. For the investors approaching retirement (approximately ages 50-65) we have assumed the threshold to be a -13.5% two-year annualized return. And for those in retirement (approximately ages 65+), we assumed a -9.0% two-year annualized return. Intended to be reflective of the views and opinions of the investment manager. The investment manager’s investment philosophy and strategy may not perform as intended and could result in loss or gain. See Important Information for additional details.
  • Employees nearing or in retirement: Conversely, individuals nearing retirement, particularly those already retired, exhibit much lower risk tolerances. For someone in retirement, even a drawdown of over 9% can result in many retirees abandoning their investment plans at precisely the wrong time. The consequences can be devastating, substantially reducing their retirement income and overall quality of life.

Behavioral finance plays a crucial role here. Psychologically, it may be challenging for individuals who have abandoned their retirement plans to re-enter the market after it rebounds. This creates a situation where they can miss out on potential gains, resulting in permanent losses and further exacerbating their financial challenges.

The impact of abandonment risk on retirement savings

The consequences of abandonment risk on retirement savings can be profound and multifaceted:

  1. Loss of long-term growth: One of the most significant impacts of abandonment risk is the loss of long-term growth potential. Abandoning contributions can severely hinder the compounding effect of investments. For instance, during the COVID-19 pandemic, those who remained invested experienced substantial returns compared to those who exited the market. Investors who withdrew during the downturn may have missed the subsequent recovery, leading to a significant opportunity cost.

2. Early withdrawal penalties: Accessing retirement funds prematurely often incurs tax penalties and fees, further diminishing savings. These costs can compound over time, resulting in a significantly reduced nest egg for retirement.

3. Reduced quality of life in retirement: Financial insecurity in later years can increase reliance on social safety nets or family support. This affects the individual's quality of life and can burden family members and society.

Strategies to mitigate retirement savings abandonment risk

Mitigating retirement savings abandonment risk requires a multifaceted approach:

Employers can significantly support retirement savings by implementing auto-re-enrollment in retirement plans, thereby proactively including employees. A welcoming and user-friendly experience is crucial, as it can help individuals easily navigate their retirement options. Additionally, incorporating behavioral nudges—such as reminders and progress tracking—can encourage consistent contributions and foster a sense of commitment. Encouragement and practical tools will empower employees to actively engage with their retirement goals.

  • Professional asset management: Engaging with asset managers who understand the complexities of retirement investing can help mitigate abandonment risk. These professionals can design portfolios that account for market volatility, mitigate risks associated with retirement investing, and keep investors on track with their long-term goals.
  • Education, awareness, and employer support: To address abandonment risk in retirement planning, it is essential to implement financial literacy programs that highlight the importance of consistent saving and provide tools for visualizing long-term goals. Understanding the effects of market fluctuations can help investors stay committed during downturns.
    • Employers can significantly support retirement savings by implementing auto-re-enrollment in retirement plans, thereby proactively including employees. A welcoming and user-friendly experience is crucial, as it can help individuals easily navigate their retirement options. Additionally, incorporating behavioral nudges—such as reminders and progress tracking—can encourage consistent contributions and foster a sense of commitment. Encouragement and practical tools will empower employees to actively engage with their retirement goals.
  • Emergency funds: Encouraging the establishment of separate savings accounts for short-term financial needs can reduce the likelihood of withdrawals from retirement accounts. A financial cushion can provide peace of mind and lessen the urgency to access retirement savings during emergencies.

The significance of tackling abandonment risk

Addressing retirement savings abandonment risk is a critical component of long-term financial planning, and financial professionals play a central role in helping investors manage their retirement strategies. This is particularly true during periods of market stress, which have been shown to disrupt contribution and investing behavior. In fact, left unaddressed, abandonment risk can lead to adverse outcomes not only for individual's retirement, but also for the broader economy, as reliance on public resources grows with an aging U.S. population. Recognizing the structural and behavioral drivers of abandonment risk, and implementing strategies to help retirees stay on track, can make all the difference in delivering successful retirement outcomes.
